behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests model judgment quality (thread-groundedness vs generic output). A stubbed or mocked model can always return a canned grounded reply and miss real failures. I considered Workflow, but rejected it because the behavior text is a single generation outcome: "The generated reply references specific points..." (not a multi-agentic sequence assertion).
behaviors.test_description: Run the real drafting model on a set of real incoming threads and assert the draft includes concrete thread-specific anchors (e.g., named topics/questions from that thread) and fails a genericity rubric.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: Contradiction detection in natural-language drafts typically depends on model/ML semantic judgment; mocking that checker would hide real misses/false negatives. I considered Workflow, but rejected it because this behavior is scoped to one check outcome: "flags claims... that contradict entries" rather than validating a multi-step autonomous loop.
behaviors.test_description: Pass drafted replies containing subtle contradictory claims against KB facts through the real consistency checker and verify contradictions are flagged while non-contradictory claims are not.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: This is a generation-quality behavior under uncertainty (abstain/disclaimer vs fabrication), which depends on real model output. A deterministic stub would mask fabrication failures. I considered Workflow, but rejected it because the behavior text asserts a single output policy: "the draft includes a disclaimer" when KB is empty.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real drafting model with retrieval returning no relevant KB entries and assert the response includes an explicit uncertainty/disclaimer statement and avoids unsupported factual claims.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: Integration dual-quote gate satisfied. Unit-floor evidence: "approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves" shows a boundary between approval-state handling and send action. Ceiling-stop evidence: "no draft is sent automatically" defines a gate condition and does not require real OS/network/subprocess or model judgment to validate. I considered System, but rejected it because the behavior text does not require validating SMTP/provider transport semantics; an in-process send adapter fake can expose premature-send bugs. I considered Agentic/Workflow, but rejected them because correctness here is approval-state gating, not LLM decision quality or multi-agentic sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: Wire approval state manager, draft record store, and send service adapter in-process. Create a draft and attempt send without approval; assert send adapter not called. Then mark explicit approval and assert exactly one send call occurs.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly requires a chained pipeline across multiple stages, including model-driven steps (generate draft, check facts) and approval handoff, on a "real email thread." This exceeds single-step Agentic and is exactly a multi-step agent pipeline boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full production-like pipeline on a real email thread fixture/source: ingest thread, run real draft generation, run real fact-consistency check, present approval state, and verify end-to-end stage outputs plus no send before explicit approval.
